title: Dog Blood Clots in Urine: Causes, Urgency Level, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Discover what blood clots in dog urine mean, the urgency level for veterinary care, and how a photo can help your vet provide a faster diagnosis.
content_html:
What are blood clots in a dog's urine?

Finding blood clots in your dog's urine can be a distressing sight. These clots usually appear as small, red, or dark-brown gelatinous clumps within the urine. This condition indicates that bleeding is occurring somewhere within the urinary system—such as the bladder, kidneys, or urethra—and the volume of blood is high enough to allow coagulation before the urine is expelled. It is often an indicator of significant inflammation or tissue damage within the urinary tract.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for blood clots in urine is Medium. While it is rarely a 'stop everything and go to the ER' moment, your dog needs to be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours. However, this becomes a high-level emergency if your dog is straining and unable to pass any urine at all, as a clot can sometimes cause a life-threatening urethral blockage. If your dog is eating, drinking, and acting normally otherwise, a same-day or next-day appointment with your regular vet is appropriate.

Why taking a photo helps triage

We highly recommend taking a clear photo of the urine and clots on the ground or in a container. Visual evidence is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage because it allows the medical team to see the size, color, and consistency of the clots. Since dogs may not provide a clean sample immediately upon arriving at the clinic, your photo ensures the vet has immediate clinical data to help determine the severity of the issue and prioritize your pet's care.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of blood clots in urine?
faqs.answer: Common causes include severe urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, prostate issues in intact males, or trauma. In older dogs, bladder tumors or polyps are also potential considerations.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for this?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like ibuprofen or aspirin, as these can be toxic and can actually worsen bleeding disorders. Wait for a veterinary-prescribed treatment plan.

faqs.question: What tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely start with a urinalysis and a physical exam. Depending on the findings, they may also recommend blood work, an X-ray to check for stones, or an ultrasound of the bladder.
